docs.title: django-app-starter
docs.text: Generate a new Django app with everything you need to start developing, testing, and publishing — batteries included.

docs.title: What You Get
docs.text:
A freshly generated app comes with:

  • CI/CD — GitHub Actions for testing, coverage, docs, releases, and PyPI publishing
  • Testing — pytest via Hatch, covering Python 3.10–3.14 × Django 5.x–6.x
  • Coverage — Coverage.py + Codecov integration
  • Documentation — Material for MkDocs, deployed to GitHub Pages
  • Code quality — pre-commit hooks with Ruff, Black, codespell, and pyupgrade
  • Releases — automated draft releases on tag push, one-click PyPI publish
  • Labels — standardised GitHub labels synced from config
  • Dependabot — automatic dependency update PRs

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
pip install copier
copier copy https://github.com/matagus/django-app-starter <your-app-name>

After generating, follow the Post-Generation Setup Guide to activate GitHub Pages, Codecov, and PyPI publishing.

See Getting Started for the full walkthrough.

docs.title: Coming Soon
docs.text:
• Versioned documentation with Read the Docs
  • Dev containers for local development
  • all-contributors support
  • GitHub Template Repository mode

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
You need copier and hatch installed:

pip install copier
pip install hatch

docs.title: Generate a New App
docs.text:
copier copy https://github.com/matagus/django-app-starter <your-app-name>

You'll be prompted for:

  • Project name — the Python package name (e.g. django-my-app)
  • Module name — the Python module name (auto-derived, e.g. django_my_app)
  • Description — a short description of what your app does
  • GitHub username — your GitHub username or org name (used in badge URLs and clone paths)

After answering the prompts, a new directory is created with your app ready to develop.

docs.title: Run the Example Project
docs.text:
The generated app includes an example_project you can use to test your app immediately:

cd <your-app-name>
hatch run project:migrate
hatch run project:createsuperuser
hatch run project:runserver

docs.title: Run the Tests
docs.text:
Run tests across all supported Python + Django version combinations:

hatch run test:test

Run tests for a specific combination (e.g. Python 3.14 + Django 6.0):

hatch run test.py3.14-6.0:test

See all available environments:

hatch env show

docs.title: Check Coverage
docs.text: hatch run test:cov

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Your app is ready to develop — but a few one-time setup steps are needed to activate the full CI/CD pipeline:

  • Post-Generation Setup Guide — GitHub Pages, Codecov, PyPI publishing
  • GitHub Actions Overview — understand what each workflow does
  • Release Process — how to cut a release and publish to PyPI

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text: Releasing a new version involves tagging a commit, letting GitHub Actions create a draft release, then publishing that draft — which triggers the PyPI upload automatically.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before your first release, make sure you've completed:

  • [ ] PyPI Publishing setup — release environment + PYPI_API_TOKEN secret

docs.title: 1. Prepare your release
docs.text:
Make sure all changes for this release are merged to main and the CI is green.

If you want to update the version explicitly, edit the version in pyproject.toml. If you're using Hatch's dynamic versioning from git tags, no manual version bump is needed.

docs.title: 2. Tag the commit
docs.text:
Create an annotated tag on the commit you want to release:

git tag v0.1.0

Use semantic versioning: v<major>.<minor>.<patch>.

docs.title: 3. Push the tag
docs.text: git push origin v0.1.0

docs.title: 4. Wait for the draft release
docs.text:
The release.yml workflow triggers on the tag push and creates a draft GitHub release. It takes about a minute to complete.

Go to your repository → Releases to see the draft.

docs.title: 5. Review and edit the draft
docs.text:
GitHub auto-generates release notes from merged pull requests. In the draft:

  • Review the auto-generated release notes
  • Edit or add any additional context
  • Verify the tag and target commit are correct

docs.title: 6. Publish the release
docs.text: Click Publish release.

docs.title: 7. PyPI upload runs automatically
docs.text:
Publishing the release triggers the publish.yml workflow, which:

  1. Checks out the code at the release tag
  2. Runs hatch build to create the distribution files
  3. Uploads to PyPI

You can monitor the upload in the Actions tab. Once complete, your package will be live at https://pypi.org/project/<your-package-name>/.

docs.title: Verifying the Release
docs.text:
After publishing, verify:

pip install <your-package-name>==0.1.0

Or check the PyPI page directly.

docs.title: Yanking a Release
docs.text:
If you need to yank a broken release from PyPI:

  1. Go to your package's PyPI page → Release history
  2. Click the version → Options → Yank this release

Yanking prevents new installs but doesn't break existing ones. It does not delete the release from GitHub.

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text: The generated app comes with five GitHub Actions workflows. Here's a complete overview.

docs.title: Workflow Summary
docs.text: Workflow File Trigger Secrets needed CI ci.yml Push/PR to main, manual CODECOV_TOKEN Docs gh-pages.yml Push to main, manual — Labels labels.yml Push to main (.github/**), manual — Release draft release.yml Push v* tag, manual — Publish to PyPI publish.yml Release published, manual PYPI_API_TOKEN

docs.title: CI (ci.yml)
docs.text:
Runs on every push and pull request to main.

Tests your package against a matrix of Python and Django versions:

  • Python: 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, 3.14
  • Django: 5.0, 5.1, 5.2, 6.0

Incompatible combinations are excluded (e.g. Django 5.0 doesn't support Python 3.13+, Django 6.0 requires Python 3.12+).

After each test run, coverage is uploaded to Codecov. Requires the CODECOV_TOKEN secret — see Codecov setup.

docs.title: Docs (gh-pages.yml)
docs.text:
Runs on every push to main.

Builds your MkDocs documentation and deploys it to the gh-pages branch, which serves your GitHub Pages site. Uses GITHUB_TOKEN (auto-provided). Requires GitHub Pages to be enabled in your repo settings — see GitHub Pages setup.

docs.title: Labels (labels.yml)
docs.text:
Runs on every push to main that changes files under .github/**.

Syncs GitHub issue/PR labels from .github/labels.toml using the labels CLI. Uses GITHUB_TOKEN (auto-provided). No setup required — see Labels.

docs.title: Release Draft (release.yml)
docs.text:
Runs when you push a tag matching v* (e.g. v1.0.0).

Creates a draft GitHub release with auto-generated release notes based on merged pull requests since the last release. The draft is not published automatically — you review and publish it manually. This is the first step in the release process.

docs.title: Publish to PyPI (publish.yml)
docs.text:
Runs when you publish a GitHub release (not just create a draft).

Builds your package using hatch build and uploads the distribution files to PyPI using the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish action. Runs in the release environment. Requires the PYPI_API_TOKEN secret — see PyPI Publishing setup.

docs.title: Release Flow
docs.text:
git tag v1.0.0 && git push origin v1.0.0
        │
        ▼
  release.yml runs
  → Draft release created on GitHub
        │
        ▼
  You review and publish the draft
        │
        ▼
  publish.yml runs
  → Package built and uploaded to PyPI

See Release Process for the full walkthrough.

docs.title: Post-Generation Setup
docs.text: After running copier copy, your app directory is ready — but a few one-time steps are needed to fully activate the GitHub Actions workflows.

docs.title: What Works Out of the Box
docs.text:
These workflows require no configuration and will work as soon as you push to GitHub:

Workflow Trigger What it does CI Push / PR to main Runs tests across Python 3.10–3.14 × Django 5.x–6.x Labels Push to main (.github/** changed) Syncs GitHub labels from .github/labels.toml Release draft Push a v* tag Creates a draft GitHub release with auto-generated notes

docs.title: What Needs Setup
docs.text:
These require a one-time manual configuration:

  • [ ] GitHub Pages — Enable in repo settings so your docs site is deployed
  • [ ] Codecov — Add CODECOV_TOKEN secret so coverage reports are uploaded
  • [ ] PyPI Publishing — Create a release environment and add PYPI_API_TOKEN secret

Complete these steps in order — GitHub Pages is the quickest and has no external dependencies.

docs.title: After Setup
docs.text:
Once everything is configured, your workflow will be:

  1. Push code → CI runs tests and uploads coverage
  2. Push code → Docs are rebuilt and deployed
  3. Tag a release → Draft release is created automatically
  4. Publish the draft → Package is built and uploaded to PyPI

See the Release Process for a complete walkthrough.

docs.title: Codecov
docs.text: The CI workflow runs coverage on every push and pull request, and uploads the results to Codecov. This gives you per-PR coverage diffs, a coverage badge, and trend graphs over time.

docs.title: 1. Create a Codecov account
docs.text: Go to codecov.io and sign in with your GitHub account.

docs.title: 2. Add your repository
docs.text:
1. In the Codecov dashboard, click + Add new repository
  2. Find and select your repository
  3. Copy the Repository Upload Token shown on the setup page

docs.title: 3. Add the secret to GitHub
docs.text:
1. Go to your GitHub repository → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
  2. Click New repository secret
  3. Name: CODECOV_TOKEN
  4. Value: paste the token from Codecov
  5. Click Add secret

That's it. The next CI run will upload coverage automatically.

docs.title: How the Workflow Uses It
docs.text:
In ci.yml, after running tests with coverage, the workflow uploads the report:

- name: Upload coverage reports to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v5
  env:
    CODECOV_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CODECOV_TOKEN }}

docs.title: Coverage Badge
docs.text: Your generated README.md already includes a Codecov badge. Once you've completed setup and the first CI run has uploaded a report, the badge will show your current coverage percentage.

docs.title: Running Coverage Locally
docs.text:
hatch run test:cov

This generates both a JSON report (used by the CI upload) and a text report printed to the terminal.

docs.title: GitHub Pages
docs.text: The generated app includes a gh-pages.yml workflow that builds your MkDocs documentation and deploys it to GitHub Pages on every push to main. You just need to enable GitHub Pages in your repo settings once.

docs.title: 1. Push your app to GitHub
docs.text: Make sure your generated app is pushed to a GitHub repository before proceeding.

docs.title: 2. Enable GitHub Pages
docs.text:
1. Go to your repository on GitHub
  2. Click Settings → Pages (in the left sidebar under "Code and automation")
  3. Under Source, select Deploy from a branch
  4. Under Branch, select gh-pages and leave the folder as / (root)
  5. Click Save

Note

The gh-pages branch is created automatically the first time the gh-pages.yml workflow runs. If it hasn't run yet, trigger it manually from the Actions tab.

docs.title: 3. Trigger the first deploy (if needed)
docs.text:
If you haven't pushed to main yet since setting up the repo:

  1. Go to Actions → Publish docs via GitHub Pages
  2. Click Run workflow → Run workflow

docs.title: 4. Find your docs URL
docs.text:
After the workflow completes, your docs will be live at:

https://<your-github-username>.github.io/<your-repo-name>/

GitHub also shows this URL in Settings → Pages once the first deployment succeeds.

docs.title: How the Workflow Works
docs.text:
The gh-pages.yml workflow sets up Python, installs the docs dependencies from pyproject.toml, and deploys using MkDocs' built-in gh-deploy command:

- name: Set up Python
  uses: actions/setup-python@v5
  with:
    python-version: "3.x"

- name: Install docs dependencies
  run: pip install ".[docs]"

- name: Deploy docs
  run: mkdocs gh-deploy --force

No secret setup is needed — the permissions: contents: write block grants the workflow permission to push to the gh-pages branch. Dependencies come from the [project.optional-dependencies] docs section in pyproject.toml.

docs.title: Customising the Docs
docs.text: Edit mkdocs.yml and the files under docs/ to add pages, change the theme, or update navigation. See the Material for MkDocs documentation for configuration options.

docs.title: GitHub Labels
docs.text: The generated app includes a labels.yml workflow that automatically synchronises your GitHub repository labels from a config file. No manual setup is required.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The workflow triggers on any push to main that touches files under .github/**:

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    paths:
      - ".github/**"

It uses the labels CLI to sync the labels defined in .github/labels.toml to your GitHub repository. The GITHUB_TOKEN used for authentication is provided automatically — you don't need to configure any secrets.

docs.title: Customising Labels
docs.text:
Labels are defined in .github/labels.toml. Each label has a name, color, and optional description:

[[labels]]
name = "bug"
color = "d73a4a"
description = "Something isn't working"

[[labels]]
name = "enhancement"
color = "a2eeef"
description = "New feature or request"

To add, remove, or rename labels:

  1. Edit .github/labels.toml
  2. Commit and push to main
  3. The labels.yml workflow runs automatically and syncs the changes

Note

Labels that exist on GitHub but are not in labels.toml are not deleted automatically. The sync command only creates or updates labels defined in the file.

docs.title: Manual Trigger
docs.text:
To apply label changes without making another commit, you can trigger the workflow manually from the Actions tab:

  1. Go to Actions → Sync Github labels
  2. Click Run workflow → Run workflow

Wait — the current labels.yml workflow doesn't include workflow_dispatch. To add manual triggering, add this to the on: block in .github/workflows/labels.yml:

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
  push:
    ...

docs.title: PyPI Publishing
docs.text: The generated app includes a publish.yml workflow that automatically builds and uploads your package to PyPI whenever you publish a GitHub Release. This requires a one-time setup.

docs.title: 1. Create a PyPI account
docs.text: Go to pypi.org and create an account if you don't have one.

docs.title: 2. Generate an API token
docs.text:
1. Log in to PyPI and go to Account settings → API tokens
  2. Click Add API token
  3. Give it a name (e.g. github-actions-<your-repo-name>)
  4. For scope, choose Entire account for the first publish; you can scope it to the project afterward
  5. Click Create token and copy the token (it starts with pypi-)

Warning

Copy the token now — PyPI will not show it again.

docs.title: 3. Create a release environment in GitHub
docs.text:
The publish.yml workflow uses a GitHub Environment called release for extra protection:

  1. Go to your GitHub repository → Settings → Environments
  2. Click New environment
  3. Name it exactly release
  4. Optionally add protection rules (e.g. require manual approval before deploying)
  5. Click Configure environment

docs.title: 4. Add the PyPI token as an environment secret
docs.text:
While still in the release environment settings:

  1. Under Environment secrets, click Add secret
  2. Name: PYPI_API_TOKEN
  3. Value: paste your PyPI token
  4. Click Add secret

docs.title: How the Workflow Works
docs.text:
publish.yml triggers when you publish a GitHub Release:

on:
  release:
    types: [published]

It then:

  1. Checks out the code at the release tag
  2. Builds the distribution using hatch build
  3. Uploads to PyPI using the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish action

The workflow runs in the release environment, which enforces any protection rules you configured.

docs.title: Trusted Publishing (OIDC) — Optional
docs.text:
The workflow already has the correct OIDC permission (id-token: write), so you can optionally use PyPI Trusted Publishing instead of an API token:

  1. On PyPI, go to your project → Settings → Publishing
  2. Add a trusted publisher: GitHub Actions, with your repo and workflow filename (publish.yml)
  3. Remove the user/password fields from the workflow and remove the PYPI_API_TOKEN secret

Trusted publishing is more secure because no token is stored anywhere.

docs.title: First Publish
docs.text: The first time you publish, PyPI will create the project under your account. Make sure the package name in pyproject.toml is available on PyPI before attempting the first release.
